title_en: The Quran is the Speech of Allah
content_id:
Doktrin bahwa Al-Qur'an adalah kalam Allah yang azali dan tidak diciptakan (Kalam Allah ghayr makhluq) adalah salah satu poin terpenting dan paling diperdebatkan secara historis dalam teologi Sunni. Al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah membahas topik ini dengan presisi, mencerminkan pendirian yang dipertahankan oleh para Imam awal — termasuk Ahmad ibn Hanbal, yang pemenjaraan dan penganiayaannya demi doktrin ini merupakan momen penentu dalam sejarah Sunni — dengan pengorbanan pribadi yang besar.

Al-Tahawi menyatakan: 'Al-Qur'an adalah kalam Allah. Ia datang dari-Nya sebagai kalam tanpa menyebutkan caranya (bila kayf). Dia menurunkannya kepada Rasul-Nya sebagai wahyu. Orang-orang beriman menegaskan ini sebagai kebenaran mutlak. Mereka yakin bahwa ia — secara hakiki — adalah kalam Allah Yang Mahatinggi, dan ia tidak diciptakan seperti kalam manusia.'

Konteks historis pernyataan ini sangat penting. Pada masa pemerintahan Khalifah Abbasiyah al-Ma'mun (198–218 H), doktrin Mu'tazilah tentang 'kemakhluqan Al-Qur'an' (khalq al-Qur'an) dipaksakan sebagai teologi negara. Kaum Mu'tazilah berargumen bahwa Al-Qur'an pasti makhluk, karena jika ia azali, maka ia akan menjadi entitas yang azali di samping Allah — yang mereka yakini akan merusak keesaan ilahi. Mereka yang menolak menegaskan kemakhluqan Al-Qur'an dikenai Mihnah (Inkuisisi). Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal dengan terkenal menanggung penjara dan cambukan daripada menegaskan kemakhluqan Al-Qur'an — suatu pendirian yang menjadi tindakan perlawanan Sunni yang paling menentukan terhadap penyimpangan teologi rasionalis.

Pendirian Sunni arus utama, yang diartikulasikan al-Tahawi, menyatakan bahwa Al-Qur'an sebagai kalam Allah yang hakiki adalah salah satu sifat-Nya yang azali (shifat dzatiyyah), bukan sesuatu yang diciptakan. Perbedaannya penting: tinta di atas halaman, suara bacaan manusia, dan kertas mushaf semuanya adalah makhluk — tetapi Al-Qur'an sebagai kalam dan firman Allah yang sesungguhnya adalah bukan makhluk. Inilah pendirian ulama Salaf dan para Imam keempat mazhab fikih.

Landasan teologisnya adalah bahwa kalam (firman) adalah sifat Allah, dan sifat-sifat-Nya — seperti ilmu, kuasa, kehendak, dan kehidupan-Nya — adalah azali dan bukan makhluk. Sebagaimana kita tidak akan mengatakan bahwa ilmu Allah adalah makhluk, kita pun tidak mengatakan bahwa kalam-Nya adalah makhluk. Al-Qur'an berulang kali menyebut dirinya sebagai 'kalam Allah' (Kalam Allah) dan menggambarkan dirinya sebagai 'dari Allah' dalam pengertian yang langsung dan tidak diciptakan.

Bagi seorang mukmin, doktrin ini memiliki implikasi yang mendalam tentang bagaimana Al-Qur'an harus diterima dan ditekuni. Jika Al-Qur'an benar-benar kalam Allah yang tidak diciptakan — sapaan langsung-Nya kepada umat manusia — maka perintah-perintahnya adalah mutlak, larangan-larangannya tidak dapat dilanggar, janji-janjinya pasti, dan petunjuknya sempurna. Tidak ada penilaian manusia yang berhak menggantinya, dan tidak ada konvensi budaya yang dapat melampaui-Nya. Menempatkan Al-Qur'an pada status kalam ilahi yang tidak diciptakan dengan demikian bukan sekadar poin teologis abstrak, melainkan fondasi otoritasnya dalam setiap bidang kehidupan.
content_en:
The doctrine that the Quran is the eternal, uncreated speech of Allah (Kalam Allah ghayr makhluq) is one of the most important and historically contested points in Sunni theology. Al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah addresses this subject with precision, reflecting the position that the early Imams — including Ahmad ibn Hanbal, whose imprisonment and persecution for this doctrine was a defining moment of Sunni history — defended at great personal cost.

Al-Tahawi states: 'The Quran is the speech of Allah. It came from Him as speech (kalam) without specifying how (bila kayf). He sent it down to His Messenger as revelation. The believers affirm this as absolute truth. They are certain that it is — in reality — the speech of Allah, the Exalted, and it is not created like the speech of human beings.'

The historical context of this declaration is essential. During the reign of the Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun (198–218 AH), the Mu'tazili doctrine of the 'createdness of the Quran' (khalq al-Quran) was imposed as state theology. The Mu'tazilah argued that the Quran must be created, because if it were eternal, it would be a co-eternal entity alongside Allah — which they believed compromised divine oneness. Those who refused to affirm this doctrine were subjected to the Mihna (Inquisition). Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal famously endured imprisonment and flogging rather than affirm the createdness of the Quran — a stand that became the defining act of Sunni resistance to rationalist theological deviation.

The mainstream Sunni position, which al-Tahawi articulates, holds that the Quran as Allah's real speech is one of His eternal attributes (sifat dhatiyan), not a created thing. The distinction is important: the ink on the page, the sound of human recitation, and the paper of the mushaf are all created — but the Quran as the actual speech and word of Allah is uncreated. This was the position of the Salaf and of the Imams of the four legal schools.

The theological underpinning is that speech (kalam) is an attribute of Allah, and His attributes — like His knowledge, power, will, and life — are eternal and uncreated. Just as we would not say that Allah's knowledge is created, we do not say that His speech is created. The Quran repeatedly refers to itself as 'the speech of Allah' (Kalam Allah) and describes itself as being 'from Allah' in a direct and uncreated sense.

For the believer, this doctrine has profound implications for how the Quran is to be received and engaged with. If the Quran is truly Allah's uncreated speech — His direct address to humanity — then its commands are absolute, its prohibitions inviolable, its promises certain, and its guidance perfect. No human judgment has the right to override it, and no cultural convention can supersede it. The elevation of the Quran to the status of uncreated divine speech is thus not merely an abstract theological point but the foundation for its authority in every domain of life.
content_ar:
The doctrine that the Quran is the eternal, uncreated speech of الله (Kalam الله ghayr makhluq) is one of the most important and historically contested points in Sunni theology. Al-Aqeedah al-Tahawiyyah addresses this subject with precision, reflecting the position that the early Imams — including Ahmad ibn Hanbal, whose imprisonment and persecution for this doctrine was a defining moment of Sunni history — defended at great personal cost.

Al-Tahawi states: 'The Quran is the speech of الله. It came from Him as speech (kalam) without specifying how (bila kayf). He sent it down to His Messenger as revelation. The believers affirm this as absolute truth. They are certain that it is — in reality — the speech of الله, the Exalted, and it is not created like the speech of human beings.'

The historical context of this declaration is essential. During the reign of the Abbasid Caliph al-Ma'mun (198–218 AH), the Mu'tazili doctrine of the 'createdness of the Quran' (khalq al-Quran) was imposed as state theology. The Mu'tazilah argued that the Quran must be created, because if it were eternal, it would be a co-eternal entity alongside الله — which they believed compromised divine oneness. Those who refused to affirm this doctrine were subjected to the Mihna (Inquisition). Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal famously endured imprisonment and flogging rather than affirm the createdness of the Quran — a stand that became the defining act of Sunni resistance to rationalist theological deviation.

The mainstream Sunni position, which al-Tahawi articulates, holds that the Quran as الله's real speech is one of His eternal attributes (sifat dhatiyan), not a created thing. The distinction is important: the ink on the page, the sound of human recitation, and the paper of the mushaf are all created — but the Quran as the actual speech and word of الله is uncreated. This was the position of the Salaf and of the Imams of the four legal schools.

The theological underpinning is that speech (kalam) is an attribute of الله, and His attributes — like His knowledge, power, will, and life — are eternal and uncreated. Just as we would not say that الله's knowledge is created, we do not say that His speech is created. The Quran repeatedly refers to itself as 'the speech of الله' (Kalam الله) and describes itself as being 'from الله' in a direct and uncreated sense.

For the believer, this doctrine has profound implications for how the Quran is to be received and engaged with. If the Quran is truly الله's uncreated speech — His direct address to humanity — then its commands are absolute, its prohibitions inviolable, its promises certain, and its guidance perfect. No human judgment has the right to override it, and no cultural convention can supersede it. The elevation of the Quran to the status of uncreated divine speech is thus not merely an abstract theological point but the foundation for its authority in every domain of life.
